title: Optimally Merge Intervals from Two Sets under Conflict Constraints

statement.markdown: Given two sets of non-overlapping intervals, merge the intervals from both sets in such a way that the conflicting pairs of intervals are minimized. Intervals are defined as [start, end] and conflict with each other if they have overlapping time slots.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains n, the size of the first set of intervals. The next n lines contain the start and end of each interval in the first set. The following line contains m, the size of the second set of intervals. The next m lines contain the start and end of each interval in the second set.
statement.outputFormat: The first line contains the total number of non-conflicting pairs of merged intervals. The next line contains the start and end of each merged interval, one interval per line.
statement.notes: The order of intervals in the output may vary. Use a greedy approach to minimize conflicts.

constraints.description: Size of first set of intervals
Size of second set of intervals

examples.explanation: Merged intervals from both sets are [1, 5] and [7, 9]

hints.text: The key is to first sort both interval sets by their end points.

editorial.title: Optimal Interval Merging
editorial.intuition: We use a greedy approach by sorting both interval sets by their end points.
editorial.bruteForce: We can iterate through each interval in both sets and check for conflicts, but this will result in inefficient time complexity.
editorial.betterSolution: Sorting both sets by end points will allow us to merge intervals in a more efficient manner.
editorial.optimalSolution: We iterate through both sorted sets of intervals and choose the one with the smallest end point while minimizing conflicts.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: This greedy approach ensures that the total number of non-conflicting pairs of intervals is maximized.
editorial.dryRun: Example run: [1, 3], [4, 5], [6, 8] and [3, 5], [7, 9] are sorted by end points and merged accordingly.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to sort the intervals by their end points
Not handling the case where intervals conflict
editorial.timeComplexity: O(n log n + m log m)

solutions.python:
from sys import stdin
from typing import List

# Read input
n = int(stdin.readline())
m = int(stdin.readline())

# Parse intervals
intervals1 = [(int(x), int(y)) for x, y in (line.split() for _ in range(n))]

intervals2 = [(int(x), int(y)) for x, y in (line.split() for _ in range(m))]

# Sort intervals by end points
intervals1.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])
intervals2.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])

class Solution:
    def mergeIntervals(self, intervals1, intervals2) -> List[tuple[int, int]]:
        result = []
        i, j = 0, 0

        while i < len(intervals1) and j < len(intervals2):
            # Merge intervals that don't conflict
            if intervals1[i][1] < intervals2[j][1]:
                result.append(intervals1[i])
                i += 1
            elif intervals2[j][1] < intervals1[i][1]:
                result.append(intervals2[j])
                j += 1
            else:
                # Merging conflicting intervals
                merged_start = min(intervals1[i][0], intervals2[j][0])
                merged_end = max(intervals1[i][1], intervals2[j][1])
                result.append((merged_start, merged_end))
                i += 1
                j += 1

        # Append any remaining intervals
        while i < len(intervals1):
            result.append(intervals1[i])
            i += 1
        while j < len(intervals2):
            result.append(intervals2[j])
            j += 1

        return result

solution = Solution()
solution.mergeIntervals(intervals1, intervals2)

# Print output
for pair in solution.mergeIntervals(intervals1, intervals2):
    print(*pair)
